frameworks.name: Close Rate to Price Multiple Ladder
frameworks.definition: A tiered rule-of-thumb mapping sales close rates to implied pricing multiples, used to diagnose whether a business is underpriced relative to demand.

frameworks.name: LTV:CAC Human-in-Loop Multiplier
frameworks.definition: A framework for setting minimum LTV-to-CAC ratio targets based on how many of three business functions (attraction, conversion, delivery) involve a human, scaling from 3:1 (all automated) to 12:1 (all human).

frameworks.name: 30-Day Cash Collected Rule
frameworks.definition: A cash-flow rule requiring that gross profit collected within the first 30 days of a new customer relationship exceeds the sum of CAC plus COGS, enabling a self-funding acquisition loop.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a sales close rate of 80% or above is a reliable signal that the business is underpriced by approximately 3 to 4x, based on data collected across 14 years and the Acquisition.com portfolio.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to businesses selling via salesperson in-person or on phone/Zoom
Assumes a functioning sales motion is in place
claims.mechanism: High close rates signal that nearly all willing buyers are converting, indicating price is below the market-clearing level; supply-demand dynamics imply demand would remain strong at a significantly higher price.

numbers.axis: Close rate as a proxy for pricing headroom

anchors.value: likely avatar or sales motion problem, not a price problem

bounded_by: SaaS companies: price must balance gross margin against growth rate simultaneously, so the rule does not apply directly
Businesses with below-30% close rates: diagnose avatar fit and sales motion before changing price

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that for service businesses (which he estimates as ~80% of all businesses), the only sustainable pricing direction is upward, because raising prices increases gross margins, which enables better talent, which builds reputation, creating a virtuous cycle; attempting to serve more people at lower prices creates the inverse vicious cycle.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to unscalable, labor-intensive service businesses
Assumes business develops sufficient demand to be selective
claims.mechanism: Higher prices produce higher gross margins; higher gross margins fund better talent compensation; better talent produces better client results; better results build reputation and allow further price increases. The inverse loop applies when prices are lowered.
bounded_by: SaaS companies: price decision must balance gross margin against growth rate

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the appropriate LTV-to-CAC ratio target for a business depends on how many of three functions (attraction, conversion, delivery) involve a human: all automated (zero humans) warrants a 3:1 minimum; one human in the loop warrants 6:1; two humans warrant 9:1; all three humans warrant 12:1.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to businesses intending to scale
These are minimums for scale, not operational targets for small steady-state businesses
claims.mechanism: Adding a human to any function introduces lumpiness and inconsistency (ramp-up time, skill degradation in new hires). Higher LTV:CAC targets provide the padding to absorb those inefficiencies without going below breakeven during scaling.

numbers.axis: Minimum LTV-to-CAC ratio by number of human-dependent functions

anchors.region: 3 humans in the loop (attraction + conversion + delivery)

bounded_by: Businesses not intending to scale large can operate at 6:1 and sustain a few million in revenue
Very large businesses with capital reserves can tolerate lower ratios temporarily

claims.text: Alex Hormozi reports that in the first year of Gym Launch, his LTV-to-CAC ratio was approximately 100:1, achieved by spending roughly $100,000 in customer acquisition and generating approximately $10 million in revenue, illustrating that arbitrage windows of 30:1 or above are real and repeatable.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: First year of Gym Launch, a specific arbitrage window
claims.mechanism: Gym Launch captured an underserved market at a moment when CAC was extremely low relative to customer lifetime value; such windows are time-limited as competition and infrastructure costs increase.

numbers.axis: LTV:CAC ratio at Gym Launch year one

bounded_by: This was a specific arbitrage window, not a sustainable steady-state ratio

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that for a business to achieve extraordinary scale, either CAC must approach zero (via massive brand or viral product) or LTV must approach infinity (via enterprise-level value per customer); one of these two extremes must be present for a truly large company.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to businesses seeking very large scale, not lifestyle businesses
claims.mechanism: The LTV:CAC ratio is fundamentally bounded by how small the numerator denominator can become; the only paths to exceptional ratios at scale are radical reduction of CAC or radical expansion of LTV.
bounded_by: Businesses content with a few million dollars of revenue need not achieve either extreme

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's rule of thumb is that contacting inbound leads by phone within 60 seconds reduces effective customer acquisition cost by approximately 4x compared to delayed contact, because prospects are warmest immediately after opting in and cool rapidly once they begin comparing alternatives.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to inbound leads who have opted in
Assumes phone as the contact channel
claims.mechanism: A prospect who has just opted in is at peak intent; delay allows them to seek competing information, reducing perceived urgency and uniqueness of the offer, directly lowering close rate and therefore increasing effective CAC.

numbers.axis: Effective CAC relative to lead response speed

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that a sales team calendar utilization of approximately 70-75% is the optimal target, with a floor of ~60% and a ceiling of ~85%, because above 85% leads book further out (reducing show rates) and salespeople cannot work their pipeline, while below 60% morale drops and commission anxiety degrades conversion quality.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to phone- or Zoom-based sales teams
Assumes salespeople carry active pipelines requiring follow-up time
claims.mechanism: Three conversion-maximizing factors require available calendar space: (1) convenient booking times increase show rates; (2) near-term bookings increase show rates relative to far-out bookings; (3) pipeline follow-up time enables salespeople to close warm leads that would otherwise expire.

numbers.axis: Sales team calendar utilization and conversion impact

anchors.value: morale decline, commission anxiety, inconsistent conversion
optimal conversion rate and pipeline management
show rates decline, pipeline abandoned, overall conversion drops despite higher absolute booking volume

bounded_by: Does not apply to checkout-page-based sales with no human salespeople

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's rule of thumb is that service businesses should target a minimum of 80% gross margin, because net margin cannot exceed gross margin, and every percentage point of gross margin below 80% compresses the operating budget available to cover sales, marketing, admin, and overhead before reaching the target net margin.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to service businesses
Manufacturing businesses have different COGS structures but same principle applies
claims.mechanism: Gross margin is the ceiling for net margin; every dollar of cost-of-delivery reduces the ceiling by one dollar. A 70% gross margin business aiming for 50% net margin has only 20% of revenue left for all other operating costs, making the target mathematically near-impossible.

numbers.axis: Gross margin percentage and resulting per-customer profit at $100 cost of delivery

bounded_by: Commodity services where customers can substitute easily: operator must first decommoditize the offer before achieving 80%+ margins

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that moving from 70% to 90% gross margin roughly doubles the gross profit per customer even though the percentage difference appears small, because the margin dollars are not linear in their effect on profit-per-customer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Illustrative at $100 cost of delivery
claims.mechanism: At a fixed cost-of-delivery, price must increase to maintain percentage margin; the price increase required to go from 70% to 90% margin is nonlinear (from $333 to $1,000), so the profit increase is proportionally much larger than the 20-percentage-point gap implies.

numbers.axis: Gross margin percentage effect on profit at $100 COGS

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's rule of thumb for the 30-day cash collected target is that gross profit collected within the first 30 days of a customer relationship must exceed the sum of customer acquisition cost (CAC) plus cost of goods sold (COGS), enabling the business to immediately reinvest in acquiring the next customer with no external capital.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to bootstrapped businesses using credit card float or self-funding
Most practical for service businesses with controllable payment timing
claims.mechanism: When 30-day gross profit exceeds CAC + COGS, the business completes a full acquisition-delivery-recoup cycle within one month, making growth self-funding and eliminating the need for external capital at any scale.
bounded_by: Manufacturing businesses with large COGS have structurally different cash cycles
VC-backed or capital-rich businesses can afford longer payback periods

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that for B2B subscription businesses, an annual customer retention rate of 80% or above is the target threshold; moving from 50% annual retention to 80% annual retention increases lifetime value per customer by approximately 2.5x at the same annual contract value.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to B2B subscription or recurring revenue businesses
claims.mechanism: LTV is approximately annual contract value divided by annual churn rate; a business with 50% retention retains customers for ~2 years on average, while an 80% retention business retains customers for ~5 years, producing 2.5x more lifetime revenue at the same CAC.

numbers.axis: Annual retention rate and its effect on customer LTV at $100/year contract value

bounded_by: B2C businesses may have different retention benchmarks

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when two competing businesses have the same CAC, the business with higher retention can outspend the competitor on customer acquisition by a factor equal to the LTV ratio, creating a durable competitive moat from retention alone.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Assumes CAC is roughly commoditized within an industry (competitive market)
claims.mechanism: A business earning 2.5x more per customer can rationally spend 2.5x more to acquire each customer while maintaining the same LTV:CAC ratio; this outbidding capability forces lower-retention competitors out of profitable acquisition channels.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's rule of thumb is that offering a prepayment option with a modest discount (e.g., buy 10 months, get 2 free) will convert approximately 15-20% of customers; adding one or more ancillary benefits (skip-the-line access, dedicated concierge, extended guarantee) alongside a moderate discount will convert 30-40% of customers to prepay.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to businesses with recurring subscription or service revenue
Assumes prepay option is actively offered at point of sale
claims.mechanism: Customers will trade future flexibility for present-value savings; adding non-monetary benefits (access, status, risk reduction) increases the perceived value of prepaying beyond the monetary discount alone, raising conversion to the offer.

numbers.axis: Prepayment conversion rate by offer structure

anchors.region: Moderate discount plus one or more ancillary benefits

claims.text: Alex Hormozi cites that third-party financing providers (specifically referencing Affirm) report a 35% increase in overall sales when financing is offered, because financing expands the buyer pool to include customers who could not make the full payment at once.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies to businesses where financing is not already the default purchase mechanism
Based on data from Affirm's internal metrics, sourced by Hormozi from senior Affirm contacts
claims.mechanism: Financing lowers the effective purchase barrier from full price to a monthly payment amount; this unlocks a previously excluded buyer segment whose income supports monthly payments but not a lump-sum purchase.

numbers.axis: Sales volume impact of offering third-party financing

bounded_by: Financing does not rescue a fundamentally broken product or service; it only amplifies what already works

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that businesses doing 30x more sales than a competitor are typically doing 30x more advertising, and that diminishing returns still produce net positive output; specifically, Acquisition.com producing ~25,000-30,000 pieces of content per year versus a $1M business producing ~365 yields the higher-output producer a proportionally larger audience, even if efficiency per piece declines.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Applies across all content and advertising channels
Assumes quality distribution is roughly similar when normalized across large samples (outliers baked in)
claims.mechanism: At large volume, the law of large numbers normalizes content quality; the top 1% and top 10% outlier pieces that drive disproportionate reach appear more frequently in proportion to total output. Diminishing returns per piece do not eliminate net gains.

numbers.axis: Annual content output and relative audience scale

anchors.value: ~25,000-30,000 pieces of content per year (~1,000x more)

bounded_by: Volume alone without minimum quality floor does not produce results; the argument assumes quality is at least consistent across the volume

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that industry average benchmarks are an inappropriate performance standard because they represent median performance of businesses that are often in debt, failing, or mediocre; using the average as the target makes average performance the ceiling.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
bounded_by: Physics-based constraints are the only real ceiling; industry averages are not

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's conversion rate benchmarks for different sales channels are: meta leads for in-person service businesses ~10%; cold webinar leads for broad markets ~2-3%; cold webinar leads for niched markets up to ~5%; web pages ~1-2%; platforms with built-in trust (e.g., Skool) ~4%; and Amazon product pages ~25% (but at a fraction of the per-sale gross profit).
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Meta leads: in-person service business context
Webinar leads: measured as opt-ins to sales, not show-to-sale
Amazon: traffic-to-purchase, but gross profit per sale is ~10x lower than direct sale
claims.mechanism: Conversion rates are primarily driven by channel trust level, pre-qualification friction, and buyer intent at point of contact; platforms with embedded trust infrastructure (Amazon, Skool) outperform cold traffic channels but at reduced margin.
bounded_by: These are rules of thumb based on Hormozi's observed portfolio data, not controlled study benchmarks

examples.text: Alex Hormozi describes the first year of Gym Launch as an extreme LTV:CAC arbitrage example: spending approximately $100,000 in advertising generated roughly $10,000,000 in revenue, yielding a 100:1 LTV:CAC ratio. He notes this pattern has occurred four times across his career, each time with ratios above 30:1, and attributes it to identifying under-served markets before competitors arrived to bid up CAC.

Alex Hormozi uses Facebook as the canonical example of a near-zero-CAC company: because the product is viral and free, Facebook acquired approximately 8 billion users at essentially zero cost per user. Even generating a few hundred dollars annually per user produces enormous total revenue when the denominator (CAC) approaches zero.

Alex Hormozi uses Salesforce as the canonical example of an extremely high-LTV company: enterprise CRM contracts can generate $1M to $5M per year per customer. Although their CAC is substantial (competitive RFP processes, long sales cycles), the LTV dwarfs the acquisition cost, creating a large positive ratio from the numerator side.

Hormozi describes a live sales team observation from Gym Launch: in high-ad-volume weeks, salespeople's calendars were full but conversion rates dropped because they had no time to work their pipeline. In low-volume weeks, the same salespeople squeezed their pipelines and maintained similar total sales. This led to the insight that pipeline management time is as important as inbound volume, underpinning the 70% calendar utilization target.

Hormozi shares a layaway negotiation story: when selling a $25,000 agency SaaS onboarding through Alan (a white-label enterprise tool), two partner prospects asked to split into payments. When Hormozi offered as many installments as they wanted, they chose $2,000/month for 12 months. He responded by scheduling their onboarding for 12 months out. They immediately agreed to pay half upfront and the balance in 30 days. The story illustrates that customers do not resist prepayment when they understand the delivery is contingent on completion of payment.

Hormozi recounts saving up all summer as a child to buy $120-160 Oakley Cyclops sunglasses (X-Men Cyclops model). The anticipation of earning and accumulating toward the purchase was more emotionally valuable than the sunglasses themselves. He never wore them after buying because he feared losing them. The story illustrates both the power of layaway-style anticipation as a customer experience and the risk of over-attaching to a purchase goal.

Hormozi describes a friend who builds complex automation machinery for large industrial clients. The machines sell for approximately $400,000 each, while the cost to build them is roughly $17,000, yielding net margins of approximately 70%. This example is used to argue that even manufacturing businesses can achieve exceptional margins when the value is in proprietary know-how rather than in raw materials or labor.

Hormozi contrasts Acquisition.com's ~25,000-30,000 annual content pieces against a $1M business producing roughly 365 pieces per year. He acknowledges he may be less efficient per piece but argues that producing 1,000 times the volume and receiving 100 times the outcome is still a positive return, illustrating the diminishing-returns-are-still-returns principle.

practices.text: Before considering lowering price when close rate is below 30%, fix avatar targeting and sales motion first; price reduction is rarely the correct lever when close rates are very low.

When setting prepayment billing dates for employed (salaried) customers, ask when they get paid and align payment dates to those days; for business-owner customers, ask when their cash flow is highest in the month and schedule accordingly.

When a customer negotiates payment terms, offer layaway framing: they begin paying now and take delivery when payment is complete, aligning their incentive to pay quickly with the business incentive to receive cash early.

Always ask for a shorter payment window even when longer installment plans are agreed upon; split a 6- or 12-month service into two or three payments within the first 30-60 days rather than spreading across the service duration.
Implement the Rule of 100 when entering any new acquisition channel: commit to 100 deliberate outreach or advertising actions per day for 100 consecutive days before evaluating channel viability.
Track the original cohort of customers separately from total customer count when measuring annual retention; growth in total customers masks churn in the original cohort and overstates retention.
Avoid using industry average margins as performance benchmarks; instead, identify the physics-based ceiling and optimize toward it.
